1:
"And after the uproar was ceased, Paul called unto him the disciples, and embraced them, and departed for to go into Macedonia." The witnesses note Paul's departure from Ephesus after the riot (ch. 19). The grammatical voices note the affectionate leave-taking. After the tumult of Demetrius had subsided, Paul gathered the disciples, embraced them, and set out for Macedonia — continuing the third missionary journey.

The witnesses note Paul's pastoral care even in departure. He did not flee in haste but called the disciples, embraced them, and exhorted them. The riot had made Ephesus dangerous, but Paul left in order, with tender farewell.

The witnesses note the providence in the move. The departure to Macedonia would lead to the revisiting of the churches, the collection for Jerusalem, and ultimately the great Miletus address (vv.17-38). The verse opens the account of Paul's final journey to Jerusalem.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: after the riot subsided, Paul embraced the Ephesian disciples and departed for Macedonia — an affectionate leave-taking, continuing the third journey. The verse displays Paul's pastoral care even in departure: not fleeing in haste but gathering and embracing the disciples. The verse marks the close of the long Ephesian ministry and the beginning of the journey that would lead to Jerusalem. The verse displays the apostle's tender bonds with the churches: even leaving a dangerous city, he took time to embrace and exhort the disciples. The departure to Macedonia would carry Paul through the churches he had founded, gathering the collection for the poor saints at Jerusalem, and toward the great farewell at Miletus. The verse, brief as it is, opens a significant section of Acts: Paul's final journey, the collection, the farewells, and the road to Jerusalem and Rome. The riot at Ephesus, far from ending Paul's work, only sent him on to new labors. The verse displays the resilience of the apostle: the tumult did not silence him but moved him to continue the mission, embracing the disciples and pressing on to Macedonia. Paul's departure marks the transition from the settled Ephesian ministry to the mobile final journey toward Jerusalem.
2:
"And when he had gone over those parts, and had given them much exhortation, he came into Greece." The witnesses note Paul's journey through Macedonia and into Greece. The grammatical voices fill out the narrative from the epistles. Barnes and Robertson note that during this period Paul met Titus (2 Corinthians 2:13; 7:5-7), wrote 2 Corinthians, and labored intensely. Ellicott notes he preached as far as Illyricum (Romans 15:19).

On "much exhortation," the witnesses note Paul's diligent ministry. He revisited the Macedonian churches (Philippi, Thessalonica, Berea), exhorting and strengthening them — not merely passing through but ministering the word.

On "into Greece," the witnesses note this means Achaia, particularly Corinth. Robertson: "Now at last the coast was clear and Paul apparently had an open door in Corinth during these three months, so completely had Titus at last done away with the opposition of the Judaizers there." Paul came to Corinth, where he would write Romans.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul went through Macedonia, exhorting the churches, and came into Greece (Achaia, particularly Corinth) — a period filled, from the epistles, with the meeting of Titus, the writing of 2 Corinthians, and labor as far as Illyricum (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott). The verse displays Paul's diligent ministry on the journey: not merely traveling but exhorting and strengthening the churches. The verse marks the revisiting of the Macedonian and Greek churches: Paul, founding and confirming, gathering the collection for Jerusalem. The verse, brief as it is, covers a rich period of Paul's life — the reconciliation with Corinth through Titus, the writing of major epistles, the wide-ranging ministry. The verse displays Luke's economy: passing quickly over a significant period that the epistles fill out. The verse displays the apostle's pastoral diligence: revisiting his churches with much exhortation, confirming them in the faith. Paul's coming into Greece, to Corinth, where he would write Romans and stay three months (v.3), marks the height of his settled ministry before the final journey to Jerusalem. The much exhortation he gave displays the goal of his revisiting — not mere travel but the strengthening of the churches he had planted.
3:
"And there abode three months. And when the Jews laid wait for him, as he was about to sail into Syria, he purposed to return through Macedonia." The witnesses note Paul's three-month stay in Greece, the Jewish plot, and his change of route. On the stay, the witnesses note Paul probably wrote Romans during this time at Corinth (Barnes, Robertson, Romans 15:25-27).

On the plot, the grammatical voices note the malice of the Corinthian Jews. Ellicott: "The malignant Jews of Corinth watched their opportunity. At Cenchreae, amid the stir and bustle of a port, they might do what they had failed to do before." Robertson: Paul's "old enemies in Corinth who had cherished all these years their defeat at the hands of Gallio" plotted to kill him, perhaps at the docks or aboard the pilgrim ship.

On the change of route, the witnesses note Paul's prudence. Calvin: "when Paul doth journey another way that he may avoid their laying in wait, he showeth that we must have regard of our life so far forth that we throw not ourselves headlong into the midst of dangers." Paul, learning of the plot, returned through Macedonia rather than sailing directly.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul abode three months in Greece (probably writing Romans, Barnes, Robertson), and when the Corinthian Jews — his old enemies from the Gallio affair (Robertson) — plotted to kill him as he was about to sail, he changed his route, returning through Macedonia. Calvin notes the prudence: avoiding the snare, having due regard for life without throwing himself into danger. The verse displays Paul's prudence amid danger: learning of the plot, he altered his plans to avoid it. The verse marks the persistence of Jewish malice and Paul's wise response: not recklessly courting death but prudently avoiding the ambush. The verse displays the balance of courage and prudence in the apostle: he would face danger when duty required, but would not needlessly expose himself. The plot, intended to kill Paul, only changed his route, giving him another visit to the Macedonian churches. The verse displays the providence that preserved Paul: the plot discovered, the route changed, the apostle preserved for further labor and for the great farewell at Miletus. Paul's prudence — avoiding the ambush while pressing on to Jerusalem — displays the wisdom of the servant of Christ: bold in duty, yet careful not to throw away life needlessly.
4:
"And there accompanied him into Asia Sopater of Berea; and of the Thessalonians, Aristarchus and Secundus; and Gaius of Derbe, and Timotheus; and of Asia, Tychicus and Trophimus." The witnesses note the company of delegates accompanying Paul. The grammatical voices note their purpose: they were delegates from the churches, carrying the collection for Jerusalem. Ellicott: "He was carrying up a large sum in trust for the churches of Judaea, and he sought to avoid even the suspicion of the malversations which the tongue of slanderers was so ready to impute to him (2 Corinthians 8:20-21). Representatives were accordingly chosen from the leading churches."

On the individuals, the witnesses note their origins and later history. Sopater of Berea (perhaps the Sosipater of Romans 16:21); Aristarchus and Secundus of Thessalonica; Gaius of Derbe and Timothy; Tychicus and Trophimus of Asia (Trophimus the Ephesian, 21:29). These represented the provinces that shared in the collection (Robertson).

The witnesses note the wisdom of the arrangement. Robertson: "It was an important event and Paul took the utmost pains to remove any opportunity for scandal in the handling of the funds." The delegates would be witnesses to the integrity of the collection.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: a company of delegates accompanied Paul — Sopater, Aristarchus, Secundus, Gaius, Timothy, Tychicus, and Trophimus — representatives of the churches carrying the collection for Jerusalem (Ellicott), that Paul might avoid any suspicion in handling the large sum (2 Corinthians 8:20-21). The delegates came from the provinces that shared in the collection (Robertson). The verse displays the wisdom of the apostle in handling the funds: appointing church representatives as witnesses to the integrity of the collection. The verse marks the gathering of the delegation for the great journey to Jerusalem: representatives of the Gentile churches, carrying their gift to the poor saints. The verse displays Paul's care for his reputation and the church's: removing any opportunity for scandal in the handling of money. The naming of the delegates, with their varied origins, displays the breadth of the collection — Macedonia, Galatia, Asia all contributing. The verse displays the unity of the churches: the Gentile congregations sending their gift to the Jewish believers, with chosen witnesses to vouch for its integrity. Paul's wisdom in the handling of the collection — appointing delegates, avoiding suspicion — is a model for all who handle the church's funds: transparency and accountability, that the ministry be not blamed.
5:
"These going before tarried for us at Troas." The brief verse notes the delegates going ahead to Troas — and the resumption of the "we" narrative. The grammatical voices note the significant "us." Robertson: "Here again we have 'us' for the first time since chapter 16 where Paul was with Luke in Philippi. Had Luke remained all this time in Philippi? We do not know, but he is with Paul now till Rome is reached." Luke rejoins the narrative.

On the delegates going before, the witnesses note the arrangement. The seven delegates went ahead to Troas, while Paul (with Luke) followed, having kept the Passover at Philippi (v.6).

On the purpose, Ellicott notes: "The disciples who went on in advance would announce St. Paul's coming to the church of Troas, and so there would be a full gathering to receive him." The advance party prepared the way.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: the delegates went ahead to Troas and waited, while the "we" narrative resumes — Luke rejoining Paul (last seen together at Philippi in ch. 16, Robertson), now to remain with him till Rome. The advance party would announce Paul's coming and prepare a full gathering (Ellicott). The verse displays the resumption of Luke's eyewitness narrative: "us" marking his presence with Paul again. The verse marks the reuniting of Paul and Luke: the beloved physician and companion, who would accompany Paul through the perils ahead to Rome. The verse displays the arrangement of the journey: the delegates going ahead, Paul and Luke following after the Passover. The brief notice, with its significant "us," marks a turning-point: Luke is again present, and the vivid eyewitness detail of the following chapters (the Eutychus miracle, the Miletus address, the storm and shipwreck) flows from his presence. The verse displays the providence of companionship: Luke, the faithful friend and physician, rejoining Paul for the final, perilous stage of his journey. The advance of the delegates to Troas, and the reuniting with Luke, set the scene for the events at Troas (vv.7-12) and the great farewell at Miletus.
6:
"And we sailed away from Philippi after the days of unleavened bread, and came unto them to Troas in five days; where we abode seven days." The witnesses note the voyage from Philippi to Troas and the seven-day stay. On "after the days of unleavened bread," the witnesses note Paul kept the Passover at Philippi. Robertson: "Paul was a Jew, though a Christian, and observed the Jewish feasts, though he protested against Gentiles being forced to do it." He kept the feast, then sailed.

On "in five days," the witnesses note the slow voyage (against contrary winds). Ellicott: "the ship had now to contend against the south-west current... and probably also against the Etesian winds." The voyage that once took three days (16:11) now took five.

On the seven-day stay, the witnesses note its purpose: to keep the Lord's Day with the church. Ellicott: "the motive for this stay was to keep the Lord's day... and to partake with the Church of what... St. Paul had already spoken of as the Lord's Supper (1 Corinthians 11:20)." The stay allowed the gathering of v.7.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, having kept the Passover at Philippi (observing the Jewish feast though a Christian, Robertson), sailed to Troas in five days (slowed by contrary winds, Ellicott) and stayed seven days — the stay allowing him to keep the Lord's Day with the church and break bread (Ellicott, v.7). The verse displays the rhythm of the journey: the Passover kept, the voyage made, the week's stay at Troas for the Lord's Day gathering. The verse marks the approach to the Eutychus miracle (vv.7-12): the seven-day stay culminating in the Lord's Day assembly. The verse displays Paul's observance of the Jewish feast (the Passover) even as a Christian — keeping it, though not binding it on the Gentiles. The seven-day stay, arranged to include the Lord's Day, displays the importance of the Christian assembly: Paul waited a week to gather with the church for worship and the breaking of bread. The verse sets the scene for the dramatic events at Troas: the long discourse, the sleeping Eutychus, the fall and the raising. Paul's keeping of the Passover and his waiting for the Lord's Day display the transition from the old to the new: the Jewish feast observed, yet the Christian assembly on the first day of the week now the center of worship.
7:
"And upon the first day of the week, when the disciples came together to break bread, Paul preached unto them, ready to depart on the morrow; and continued his speech until midnight." The witnesses note the Lord's Day assembly, the breaking of bread, and Paul's long discourse — key evidence for Christian worship on Sunday. On "the first day of the week," the witnesses note the Christian sabbath. Gill cites Justin Martyr on Sunday worship; the puritan Henry: "they came together upon the first day of the week, which they called the Lord's day (Revelation 1:10), the Christian sabbath, celebrated to the honour of Christ and the Holy Spirit, in remembrance of the resurrection of Christ, and the pouring out of the Spirit, both on the first day of the week."

On "to break bread," the witnesses note the Lord's Supper (with the agape). Ellicott and Robertson note the breaking of bread as the synonym for the Lord's Supper (1 Corinthians 10:16). The assembly gathered for worship and the Eucharist.

The witnesses note the long discourse. Robertson notes the meeting lasted from evening till dawn, with the Eucharist and Paul's preaching. Calvin notes the disciples' eagerness to hear Paul, knowing he was departing. The verse establishes the Christian custom of Lord's Day worship.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: on the first day of the week the disciples gathered to break bread, and Paul preached till midnight — key evidence for Christian worship on Sunday, the Lord's Day, the Christian sabbath kept in remembrance of the resurrection and Pentecost (Henry, Gill). The "breaking of bread" is the Lord's Supper with the agape (Ellicott, Robertson, 1 Corinthians 10:16). The verse displays the established pattern of Christian worship: the Lord's Day assembly for the word and the Supper. The verse marks the change from the Jewish sabbath to the Christian Lord's Day: the disciples gathering on the first day of the week, in honor of the risen Christ. The verse displays the centrality of the assembly: gathering for the breaking of bread and the preaching of the word. Paul's long discourse, lasting till midnight (and beyond, v.11), displays both his eagerness to instruct the church before departing and the disciples' eagerness to hear him. The verse is a foundational text for the Christian observance of Sunday: the first day of the week, the Lord's Day, the day of the resurrection, now the day of Christian worship and the breaking of bread. The transition from sabbath to Lord's Day, displayed here, marks the new covenant's worship, centered on the risen Christ.
8:
"And there were many lights in the upper chamber, where they were gathered together." The witnesses note the many lamps in the upper room. On the purpose of mentioning the lights, the witnesses note the answer to slander. Barnes: "It, however, meets one of the slanders of the early enemies of Christianity, that Christians in their assemblies were accustomed to extinguish all the lights, and to commit every kind of abomination." Wesley: "To prevent any possible scandal." The lights testified to the openness and purity of the assembly.

On the practical effect, the witnesses note the heat and closeness. Ellicott: the lamps are mentioned "partly as accounting for the sleep of Eutychus by the heat and closeness of the room." Robertson notes the flickering, smoking oil lamps. The many lights, in the crowded upper room, made the air close.

On the upper chamber, the witnesses note the customary place of assembly (Acts 1:13). The third-floor room, removed from the street, was used for the gathering. The verse sets the scene for the Eutychus incident (v.9).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: there were many lights in the upper chamber — mentioned partly to refute the slander that Christians met in darkness for wickedness (Barnes, Wesley), and partly to account for Eutychus's sleep by the heat and closeness (Ellicott, Robertson). The verse displays the openness and purity of the Christian assembly: many lights, nothing hidden. The verse marks the setting of the Eutychus miracle: the crowded, well-lit, but close and warm upper room. The verse displays both an answer to calumny (the lights testifying to the purity of the gathering) and a realistic detail (the heat that overcame the young man). The mention of the many lights, far from incidental, serves Luke's purpose: to show the assembly as open and honest, and to explain the drowsiness of Eutychus. The verse displays the vivid, eyewitness quality of Luke's narrative: the detail of the lamps, remembered by one who was present. The upper chamber, lit by many lamps and crowded with disciples, is the scene of the dramatic events to follow — the long discourse, the sleeping youth, the fall, and the raising. The lights that testified to the purity of the assembly also, by their heat, set the stage for the miracle.
9:
"And there sat in a window a certain young man named Eutychus, being fallen into a deep sleep: and as Paul was long preaching, he sunk down with sleep, and fell down from the third loft, and was taken up dead." The witnesses note the fall of Eutychus and debate whether he truly died. On the death, the witnesses divide. Barnes holds he truly died: "the obvious and therefore the safest interpretation is, that he was actually killed by the fall, and was miraculously restored to life." Robertson notes Luke the physician's view: "Was taken up dead... Luke does not say 'as' or 'as if.' The people considered him dead and Luke the physician seems to agree."

Ellicott, however, leaves room for apparent death: "it may be questioned, looking to St. Paul's words, 'his life is in him,' whether more than apparent death is meant. He was to all appearance dead — would have died but for the prayer of the Apostle." The cause was the shock of the fall.

The Reformed Calvin defends Eutychus from blame and affirms the death: "I see no cause why some interpreters should so sore and sharply condemn the drowsiness of the young man... Luke doth in plain words acquit Eutychus, when he saith that he fell down, being overcome with deep sleep after midnight." Calvin holds he truly died, the Lord using it to confirm the faith.

The witnesses agree: Eutychus, overcome by deep sleep during Paul's long preaching, fell from the third floor and was taken up dead. They divide on whether he truly died: most (Barnes, Calvin, Robertson — Luke the physician's view) hold a real death and miraculous restoration; Ellicott leaves room for apparent death (a swoon from the shock of the fall), citing "his life is in him." Calvin defends Eutychus from the charge of culpable drowsiness — he was overcome by nature after midnight, not slothful. The verse displays the dramatic accident: the sleeping youth falling to his death. The verse marks the occasion of the miracle (v.10): the death (or apparent death) of Eutychus, to be reversed by Paul. The verse displays both the realistic detail (the heat, the long discourse, the deep sleep, the fall) and the question of the miracle's nature. The debate — true death or swoon — turns on Paul's words "his life is in him" (v.10), which suit either a restoration or a discovery that he yet lived. The verse displays Luke the physician's careful observation: "taken up dead," with no qualifying "as if." Whether a true resurrection or a recovery from apparent death, the event confirmed the faith of the church and sealed Paul's last sermon at Troas.
10:
"And Paul went down, and fell on him, and embracing him said, Trouble not yourselves; for his life is in him." The witnesses note Paul's raising of Eutychus, in the manner of Elijah and Elisha. On "fell on him," the witnesses note the prophetic gesture. Barnes: "Probably stretching himself on him as Elisha did on the Shunammite's son (2 Kings 4:33-35)... an act of tenderness and compassion." Ellicott and Calvin compare Elijah (1 Kings 17:21).

On "his life is in him," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes: "He is restored to life... Life was restored to him as Paul spoke." Calvin: "When he saith that his soul is in him, he doth not deny that he was dead... but the meaning of these words is, that his life was restored through the grace of God." Robertson notes the phrase could suit either a swoon or a restoration.

The Reformed Calvin notes Paul's purpose: "Paul took great care principally for this cause, lest that sorrowful event should shake the faith of the godly... the Lord did, as it were, seal up and establish that last sermon which Paul made at Troas." The miracle confirmed the faith and Paul's preaching.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul went down, fell on Eutychus (as Elijah and Elisha had done, 1 Kings 17:21; 2 Kings 4:34), embraced him, and declared "his life is in him" — restoring him to life through prayer (Barnes), or, on Calvin's reading, his life restored by God's grace (not denying the death). Calvin notes Paul's purpose: lest the sorrowful event shake the church's faith, the Lord sealed Paul's last Troas sermon with the miracle. The verse displays the apostolic miracle: Paul raising the dead youth, in the manner of the prophets. The verse marks the great sign at Troas: the restoration of Eutychus, confirming Paul's ministry and the church's faith. The verse displays the tenderness and power of the apostle: falling on the youth, embracing him, and declaring his life restored. The gesture, echoing Elijah and Elisha, links Paul to the prophets; and the result — the youth alive — sealed the faith of the church. The verse displays the providence that turned a tragedy into a confirmation: the death that might have shaken the disciples became, through the miracle, a seal upon Paul's preaching. Whether a raising from death or a restoration from a swoon, the event displayed God's power and grace, comforting the church and establishing the word Paul had preached.
11:
"When he therefore was come up again, and had broken bread, and eaten, and talked a long while, even till break of day, so he departed." The witnesses note Paul's resumption of the assembly after the miracle. On "broken bread, and eaten," the witnesses divide on whether this is the Eucharist or a meal. Robertson: "Probably the Eucharist." Barnes: "Had taken refreshment. As this is spoken of Paul only, it is evidently distinguished from the celebration of the Lord's Supper." Ellicott connects it to the Lord's Supper interrupted by the accident.

On "talked a long while," the witnesses note the change of tone. Clarke: the word here (homilesas, familiar conversation) differs from the solemn discourse (dielegeto) of v.7. Robertson: this second discourse, till dawn, was "more informal and conversational." After the miracle, Paul resumed in a more familiar manner.

On "till break of day," the witnesses note the all-night gathering. The whole service lasted some seven or eight hours (Ellicott), the breaking of bread and the conversation continuing till dawn, when Paul departed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: after the miracle, Paul came up again, broke bread and ate, and talked till daybreak, then departed. The "breaking of bread" is probably the Eucharist (Robertson, Ellicott — the Supper interrupted by the accident, now observed), or a refreshing meal (Barnes); the "talking" was a more familiar conversation than the earlier solemn discourse (Clarke, Robertson). The verse displays the resumption of the assembly after the miracle: the breaking of bread and the long conversation till dawn. The verse marks the completion of the Lord's Day gathering: the Supper observed, the discourse resumed, the night spent in worship and instruction. The verse displays the eagerness of both Paul and the disciples: the long night of breaking bread, eating, and conversing till dawn, before Paul's departure. The verse displays the richness of the Christian assembly: worship, the Supper, preaching, and familiar conversation, all in one extended gathering. The miracle, far from ending the assembly, was followed by the calm resumption of the breaking of bread and the discourse — Paul's composure displaying his confidence in the restoration. The all-night gathering, ending at dawn with Paul's departure, displays the depth of fellowship between the apostle and the church at Troas, on the eve of his final journey.
12:
"And they brought the young man alive, and were not a little comforted." The brief verse notes the joy at Eutychus's restoration. The grammatical voices note the emphasis on "alive." Robertson: "Knowling holds that 'living' here is pointless unless he had been dead. He had been taken up dead and now they brought him living." The youth, restored, was brought alive.

On "not a little comforted," the witnesses note the litotes (a great comfort). Barnes: "By the fact that he was alive; perhaps also strengthened by the evidence that a miracle had been wrought." The church was greatly comforted, both by the youth's life and by the confirmation of Paul's ministry.

The verse marks the joyful close of the Troas incident: the dead (or apparently dead) youth brought alive, the church comforted. The miracle sealed Paul's last sermon at Troas and strengthened the disciples on the eve of his departure.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: they brought the young man alive, and were greatly comforted (the litotes "not a little" marking a great comfort, Barnes) — the emphasis on "alive" confirming he had been dead (Robertson). The verse displays the joyful outcome: the restored youth, the comforted church. The verse marks the close of the Eutychus incident: the miracle accomplished, the youth alive, the disciples strengthened. The verse displays the double comfort: the youth's life and the confirmation that a miracle had been wrought (Barnes). The verse, brief as it is, completes the dramatic episode: the long discourse, the fatal fall, the raising, and now the joy at the youth brought alive. The miracle, sealing Paul's ministry, comforted the church on the eve of his departure — a parting gift of grace. The verse displays the providence that turned tragedy into joy: the death that grieved the assembly became, through the miracle, an occasion of great comfort and a confirmation of the gospel. The youth brought alive, and the church comforted, mark the gracious close of Paul's visit to Troas. The miracle that restored Eutychus also strengthened the faith of the disciples, sending them on with comfort as Paul departed to face the perils ahead.
13:
"And we went before to ship, and sailed unto Assos, there intending to take in Paul: for so had he appointed, minding himself to go afoot." The witnesses note Paul's choice to go by land to Assos while the others sailed. The puritan Henry and the witnesses note Paul's purpose in going on foot. Some suppose he wished to be alone, perhaps for prayer and meditation, or to visit disciples along the way; others, that he wished to avoid the rough sea passage around Cape Lectum.

The grammatical voices note the arrangement. The companions sailed around the promontory to Assos, while Paul took the shorter land route, intending to rejoin them there. The journey by foot was about twenty miles.

The Reformed Calvin and others note Paul's desire for solitude. Whatever the reason, Paul chose to walk alone to Assos, perhaps for private devotion before the trials ahead, rejoining the ship there. The verse displays the apostle's deliberate arrangement of the journey.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the companions sailed to Assos while Paul went on foot, intending to rejoin them there — by his own appointment (Henry). The reasons are conjectured: perhaps for solitude and prayer, perhaps to visit disciples, perhaps to avoid the sea passage. The verse displays Paul's deliberate arrangement: choosing the land route while the others sailed. The verse marks a small but vivid detail of the journey: Paul walking alone to Assos, rejoining the ship there. The verse displays the apostle's desire, perhaps, for solitude — a time of private devotion before the perils of Jerusalem. The eyewitness detail (Paul going afoot, the others by ship) displays Luke's careful narrative. Whatever Paul's reason — meditation, ministry along the way, or avoiding the rough passage — the verse shows him ordering his journey thoughtfully, choosing time alone before rejoining his company. The verse, with its vivid particularity, marks the human texture of the journey: the apostle walking the coastal road to Assos while his friends sailed around the cape, all to meet again and continue together toward Jerusalem. Paul's solitary walk displays the contemplative side of the active apostle, seeking, it may be, communion with God before the trials that awaited him.
14:
"And when he met with us at Assos, we took him in, and came to Mitylene." The brief verse notes Paul rejoining the ship at Assos. The grammatical voices note the meeting. Robertson: "as soon as Paul came near or began to meet us, we picked him up." Paul, having walked from Troas, met the ship at Assos as arranged.

On Mitylene, the witnesses note it was the capital of Lesbos. The ship, having taken Paul aboard, sailed on to Mitylene, about thirty miles from Assos — an easy day's sail.

The verse marks the resumption of the joint journey: Paul rejoining his companions, the company sailing on toward Jerusalem. The brief notice continues the careful itinerary of the voyage.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: Paul met the ship at Assos (as arranged, having walked from Troas, Robertson), and they took him aboard and came to Mitylene (the capital of Lesbos, an easy day's sail). The verse displays the resumption of the joint journey: Paul rejoining his companions after his solitary walk. The verse marks a stage in the careful itinerary: Assos to Mitylene, the company reunited and sailing on. The verse, brief as it is, displays Luke's precise record of the voyage — the meeting at Assos, the sail to Mitylene. The verse displays the orderly progress of the journey toward Jerusalem: Paul and his companions together again, moving down the coast of Asia Minor. The reuniting at Assos, after Paul's time alone, marks the return to the fellowship of the journey: the apostle and his friends, the delegates with the collection, all sailing toward the holy city. The verse continues the vivid, day-by-day account of the voyage, displaying Luke's eyewitness care. The simple notice — Paul taken aboard at Assos, the ship coming to Mitylene — advances the narrative toward the great farewell at Miletus (vv.17-38) and the perils awaiting at Jerusalem. The journey, carefully recorded, presses on stage by stage toward its appointed end. The verse displays the quiet faithfulness of the record: even the smallest stage of the voyage is noted, the apostle taken aboard at the appointed place, the ship coasting on. The reuniting at Assos, after Paul's solitary walk, restores him to the fellowship of his companions for the remainder of the journey toward Jerusalem and the bonds the Spirit had foretold.
15:
"And we sailed thence, and came the next day over against Chios; and the next day we arrived at Samos, and tarried at Trogyllium; and the next day we came to Miletus." The witnesses note the stages of the voyage down the coast. The grammatical voices note the day-by-day progress. Ellicott: "After the usual manner of the Mediterranean navigation of the time, the ship put into harbour, where it was possible, every evening." The ship sailed past Chios, Samos, Trogyllium, to Miletus.

On the islands, the witnesses note their classical associations (Chios, Samos), but observe these meant nothing to Paul intent on his mission (Ellicott). The voyage hugged the coast, anchoring each night.

On Miletus, the witnesses note it was the port near Ephesus (about thirty miles south). Robertson: "once the chief city of the Ionian Greeks, was now quite eclipsed by Ephesus." Here Paul would summon the Ephesian elders (v.17).

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ship sailed down the coast — past Chios, to Samos, Trogyllium, and Miletus — the day-by-day progress of Mediterranean navigation, anchoring each night (Ellicott). Miletus, the port near Ephesus (about thirty miles south), once chief of the Ionian cities but now eclipsed by Ephesus (Robertson), is where Paul would summon the elders. The verse displays the careful itinerary: the stages of the voyage toward Jerusalem. The verse marks the arrival at Miletus, the scene of the great farewell address (vv.17-38). The verse displays Luke's precise eyewitness record: the islands passed, the harbors made, the days counted. The classical associations of the islands (Chios, Samos) meant nothing to the apostle intent on reaching Jerusalem (Ellicott). The verse, brief as it is, advances the narrative to Miletus, the pivotal stop where Paul, unable to visit Ephesus (v.16), would call the elders to him for his moving farewell. The day-by-day account displays the steady progress of the journey, the ship coasting down the shore of Asia Minor toward the appointed meeting at Miletus and the perils beyond at Jerusalem. The arrival at Miletus sets the scene for one of the most significant passages in Acts: Paul's pastoral charge to the Ephesian elders.
16:
"For Paul had determined to sail by Ephesus, because he would not spend the time in Asia: for he hasted, if it were possible for him, to be at Jerusalem the day of Pentecost." The witnesses note Paul's decision to pass by Ephesus and hasten to Jerusalem. On "sail by Ephesus," the witnesses note this means to pass it without stopping. Ellicott: "he had decided to continue his voyage without going to Ephesus — to pass it by." A visit to Ephesus would have delayed him (Robertson notes the riot was only a year past).

On the haste to Jerusalem, the witnesses note Paul's reasons. Ellicott: Pentecost "was the Feast that attracted most pilgrims from all parts of the world, and therefore gave most scope for his work as an Apostle." Calvin: "he set before his eyes the edifying of the Church... that he might show to the faithful that the kingdom of Christ was enlarged." Paul hastened to present the collection and reach the great assembly.

The witnesses note the wisdom of summoning the elders to Miletus instead. Ellicott: by remaining at Miletus, Paul could embark at any moment, avoiding the delays and dangers of a visit to Ephesus. The verse explains the Miletus meeting (v.17).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul determined to sail past Ephesus (not stopping, to avoid delay, Ellicott, Robertson) because he hastened to be at Jerusalem for Pentecost — the feast that gathered the most pilgrims, giving scope for his work and the presentation of the collection (Ellicott, Calvin). By summoning the elders to Miletus rather than visiting Ephesus, he could embark at any moment (Ellicott). The verse displays Paul's purposeful haste: pressing toward Jerusalem for the great feast, not delaying in Asia. The verse marks the reason for the Miletus meeting: Paul, unable to spare time for Ephesus, called the elders to him. The verse displays the apostle's strategic mind: reaching Jerusalem for Pentecost, when the assembly was greatest and the collection most fittingly presented. The haste, far from neglect of Ephesus, was the wisdom of one who would not lose the opportunity of the feast. Calvin notes Paul's motive was not legal observance of the feast but the edifying of the church — the enlarging of Christ's kingdom, the gaining of strangers, the silencing of slander. The verse displays the providence and purpose in Paul's journey: hastening to Jerusalem, yet not neglecting the Ephesian elders, whom he summoned for the great farewell. Paul's determination sets the scene for the Miletus address.
17:
"And from Miletus he sent to Ephesus, and called the elders of the church." The witnesses note Paul's summoning of the Ephesian elders. The grammatical voices note the distance (about thirty miles). Despite passing by Ephesus, Paul would not leave without addressing its leaders, so he sent for the elders to come to Miletus.

Chrysostom marks the care: "See him, hasting to sail by, and yet not overlooking them, but taking order for all. Having sent for the rulers, through those he discourses to them (the Ephesians)." Paul, unable to visit, summoned the leaders to convey his charge to the whole church.

On "the elders," the witnesses note these are the same called "bishops" (overseers) in v.28. Calvin and others note the identity of elder and bishop. The elders, rulers of the Ephesian church, came at Paul's call to receive his farewell charge.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, from Miletus, sent for the elders of the Ephesian church (about thirty miles away) — not overlooking them despite passing by Ephesus, but taking order for all (Chrysostom), conveying his charge to the whole church through its leaders. The elders are the same called "bishops" in v.28 (the identity of elder and bishop, Calvin). The verse displays Paul's pastoral care: unable to visit Ephesus, he summoned its elders to receive his farewell. The verse marks the convening of the great Miletus address: Paul calling the Ephesian leaders to him for his moving charge (vv.18-35). The verse displays the apostle's devotion to the church he had served three years: he would not depart without a final word to its leaders. The summoning of the elders, at cost of time and effort, displays the importance of the farewell to come. The verse sets the scene for one of the most significant passages in Acts: Paul's pastoral charge to the elders, his review of his ministry, his warnings of wolves, and his tender farewell. The elders' coming, at Paul's call, displays their reverence for the apostle and his care for them. The verse opens the great Miletus discourse — Paul's last words to the church at Ephesus, conveyed through its elders, a model of pastoral love and charge.
18:
"And when they were come to him, he said unto them, Ye know, from the first day that I came into Asia, after what manner I have been with you at all seasons," The witnesses note the opening of Paul's address — his appeal to their knowledge of his conduct. Chrysostom marks the humility: "finding himself under a necessity of saying certain great things about himself, he tries to make the least he can of it... he calls the hearers themselves as witnesses." Paul appeals to what they themselves knew of his manner of life.

The grammatical voices note the example of the faithful minister. Paul reviews his ministry not in boasting but as a pattern for the elders to follow. He had been with them "at all seasons" — through the whole of his Ephesian ministry.

The Reformed Calvin notes the purpose: "it was very expedient that the pattern which was set before them by God of them to be followed should be always before their eyes." Paul sets his own example before the elders, that they might imitate it in their own ministry.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul opened his address by appealing to the elders' own knowledge of his manner of life from the first day in Asia — not boasting but, with humility, calling them as witnesses (Chrysostom), setting his example before them as a pattern to follow (Calvin). The verse displays the opening of the great address: Paul's appeal to his known conduct as a model for the elders. The verse marks the beginning of Paul's review of his ministry: the faithful pattern he sets before the Ephesian leaders. The verse displays the humility of the apostle: speaking of himself only by necessity, and making the least of it, appealing to their own testimony. The review of his ministry, far from self-praise, is a pastoral charge: Paul shows the elders how a minister ought to live and serve, that they might do likewise. The verse displays the value of example in ministry: Paul's own conduct, known to the elders, becomes the standard for theirs. The appeal to "at all seasons" displays the consistency of Paul's ministry — through the whole of his time at Ephesus, in good and ill. The verse opens the moving farewell with the apostle's humble yet authoritative appeal to his known and faithful example, the foundation of the charge to follow.
19:
"Serving the Lord with all humility of mind, and with many tears, and temptations, which befell me by the lying in wait of the Jews:" The witnesses note Paul's humble, tearful, tried service. On "all humility of mind," Chrysostom marks the foundation: "Observe, what most becomes rulers: hating pride... This (humility) is the groundwork of all that is good." Paul served with humility, the chief grace of a minister.

On "many tears," the witnesses note Paul's affectionate concern. He served not coldly but with tears — weeping over the lost, over the dangers, over the church. The tears displayed his deep love.

On "temptations by the lying in wait of the Jews," the witnesses note his trials. Chrysostom: "I have been evil entreated, but it was with you: and what is indeed the grievous part of the business, at the hands of Jews." Paul served amid plots and persecutions, yet faithfully.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul served the Lord with humility (the groundwork of all good in a ruler, Chrysostom), with many tears (his affectionate concern for the church), and amid temptations from Jewish plots — serving faithfully through trials. The verse displays the character of Paul's ministry: humble, tearful, tried. The verse marks the first element of Paul's review: the manner of his service — lowly, loving, and steadfast under persecution. The verse displays the model of the faithful minister: humility before God and men, tears of love and concern, and perseverance amid trials. Paul's humility, far from weakness, was the foundation of his ministry; his tears displayed his heart; his endurance under Jewish plots displayed his faithfulness. The verse displays the cost of Paul's service: not ease but trials, not pride but lowliness, not coldness but tears. The elders are shown the pattern they must follow: serving the Lord with humility, with love that weeps, and with steadfastness under persecution. The verse displays the affectionate and afflicted ministry of the apostle — a ministry of humility and tears, faithful through every plot of the enemy. Paul's example sets the standard for all who would serve the Lord: lowliness, love, and endurance.
20:
"And how I kept back nothing that was profitable unto you, but have shewed you, and have taught you publickly, and from house to house," The witnesses note Paul's full and diligent teaching. On "kept back nothing profitable," Chrysostom marks the balance: "ungrudging fulness, unshrinking promptness... For as the hiding some things would have been like grudging, so the saying all things would be folly. This is why he adds, 'that was profitable unto you.'" Paul withheld nothing useful, though he taught wisely.

On "publickly, and from house to house," the witnesses note the thoroughness. Chrysostom: "the exceeding toil, the great earnestness and endurance." Paul taught not only in public assembly but went from house to house, reaching individuals. The pastoral diligence is a model for ministers.

The Reformed Calvin notes the lesson for pastors: they must deliver "whatsoever is revealed in the Scripture... plainly and without guile." Paul withheld nothing profitable, teaching the whole counsel of God (v.27) both publicly and privately.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul kept back nothing profitable but taught fully, publicly and from house to house — ungrudging fullness joined to wise discretion (teaching all that was profitable, not folly, Chrysostom), with great toil and earnestness, reaching both the assembly and individuals. Calvin draws the lesson: pastors must deliver all that Scripture reveals, plainly and without guile. The verse displays the thoroughness of Paul's teaching: full, diligent, both public and private. The verse marks a key element of the pastoral pattern: withholding nothing profitable, teaching the whole counsel of God. The verse displays the model of the faithful teacher: not selective from cowardice or favor, but declaring all that profits; and not content with public preaching, but going house to house to reach individuals. Paul's diligence — public and private, full and discreet — is the standard for the elders. The verse displays the dual mode of ministry: the public assembly and the personal visitation, both necessary for the care of souls. Paul withheld nothing useful, yet taught wisely (not all things, but all profitable things); and he labored not only in the pulpit but in the homes. The verse displays the comprehensive, diligent ministry the elders are charged to imitate — full in doctrine, thorough in method, reaching all the flock.
21:
"Testifying both to the Jews, and also to the Greeks, repentance toward God, and faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the substance of Paul's preaching: repentance and faith, to Jew and Greek alike. On the breadth, the witnesses note Paul preached to all. Chrysostom: "Not (addressing myself) to you alone." He testified to both Jews and Greeks, making no distinction.

On "repentance toward God, and faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the twofold sum of the gospel. Chrysostom: "both by reason of their works... 'repentance towards God,' and, by reason that they knew not the Son... 'and faith in the Lord Jesus.'" Repentance and faith are the two essential responses to the gospel.

The Reformed Calvin and Barnes note this is the heart of the apostolic message. Barnes: repentance toward God for sin, and faith in Christ for salvation — the two inseparable elements of conversion. Paul's preaching, to all alike, centered on these.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul testified to both Jews and Greeks (making no distinction, Chrysostom) the twofold sum of the gospel — repentance toward God and faith toward the Lord Jesus Christ. These are the two essential responses: repentance for sin (toward God) and faith for salvation (in Christ), inseparable in conversion (Barnes, Chrysostom). The verse displays the substance of Paul's preaching: repentance and faith, the heart of the gospel, preached to all. The verse marks the content of the apostolic message: not mere doctrine but the call to repent and believe. The verse displays the universality of the gospel: preached to Jew and Greek alike, with no partiality. The twofold message — repentance and faith — is the standard of all gospel preaching: turning from sin to God, and trusting in Christ for salvation. Paul's testimony, to all peoples, centered on these inseparable graces. The verse displays the model the elders must follow: preaching repentance and faith, to all without distinction. Repentance toward God and faith toward Christ are the two pillars of conversion, the heart of the gospel Paul preached and the elders must preach. The verse displays the essential, universal message of the apostolic ministry — the call to repent and believe, addressed to every soul.
22:
"And now, behold, I go bound in the spirit unto Jerusalem, not knowing the things that shall befall me there:" The witnesses note Paul's constrained journey to Jerusalem — and debate "bound in the spirit." On the phrase, the witnesses divide. Gill, Barnes, Clarke, and Calvin favor the Holy Spirit: Barnes: "Strongly urged or constrained by the influences of the Holy Spirit." Calvin: "the inward force and motion of the Spirit." Paul went under the Spirit's compulsion.

Ellicott and Robertson, however, favor Paul's own spirit (in communion with the Divine Spirit). Ellicott: "the latter seems the more probable, subject... to the reservation that the word is used because it points to that part of his being which was most in communion with the Divine Spirit." Robertson: "His own spirit was under the control of the Holy Spirit (Romans 8:16) and the sense does not differ greatly."

On "not knowing the things that shall befall me," the witnesses note Paul's uncertainty as to particulars. He knew bonds awaited (v.23) but not the specific outcome. Calvin: "he speaketh doubtfully at the first of set purpose, that he may soften that which was about to be more bitter."

The witnesses agree: Paul went "bound in the spirit" to Jerusalem, not knowing the particulars awaiting him. They divide on the phrase: some (Gill, Barnes, Clarke, Calvin) read it of the Holy Spirit's compulsion; others (Ellicott, Robertson) of Paul's own spirit under the Spirit's control — the sense not greatly differing (Robertson), Paul's spirit being most in communion with the Divine. Paul knew bonds awaited but not the specific outcome (Calvin: speaking doubtfully to soften the bitter). The verse displays Paul's constrained, obedient journey: drawn to Jerusalem by the Spirit, regardless of danger. The verse marks the turning of the address toward Paul's coming sufferings: his resolute going, bound by the Spirit, into uncertain peril. The verse displays the obedience of the apostle: following the Spirit's leading even toward bonds and affliction. The debate over "bound in the spirit" — Paul's spirit or the Holy Spirit — does not change the substance: Paul was under a divine constraint, his will limited by the Spirit's leading (a "necessity," 1 Corinthians 9:16). The verse displays the model of obedience: going where the Spirit leads, even into danger, not knowing the outcome but trusting God. Paul's resolute journey to Jerusalem, bound by the Spirit, displays the surrender of the servant of Christ to the divine will.
23:
"Save that the Holy Ghost witnesseth in every city, saying that bonds and afflictions abide me." The witnesses note the Spirit's testimony of coming bonds. On "the Holy Ghost witnesseth in every city," the witnesses note this was through the prophets. Ellicott: "this can hardly refer to mere internal previsions of the future, but implies... predictions uttered by the mouth of prophets, such as that which was afterwards spoken by Agabus (Acts 21:11)." In every city, prophets foretold Paul's bonds.

The grammatical voices note the contrast with v.22. Robertson: the mention of "the Holy Spirit" specifically here (v.23) is "in contrast to his own spirit" (v.22). The Holy Spirit testified, distinct from Paul's own constraint.

On the nature of inspiration, Ellicott notes a valuable lesson: "The prophets... were truly inspired, as far as their prevision of the future was concerned, and yet that inspiration did not make them infallible advisers." The prophets foretold the bonds truly, but Paul rightly judged he must still go.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Holy Spirit testified in every city, through the prophets (Ellicott, as Agabus did, 21:11), that bonds and afflictions awaited Paul — the specific mention of the Holy Spirit here contrasting with Paul's own spirit in v.22 (Robertson). Ellicott draws a lesson on inspiration: the prophets truly foresaw the bonds, yet were not infallible advisers, for Paul rightly judged he must still go. The verse displays the divine forewarning: the Spirit testifying of Paul's coming sufferings. The verse marks the certainty of the bonds: not the particulars (v.22) but the general fact, attested in every city. The verse displays the courage of the apostle: knowing bonds awaited, yet pressing on. The Spirit's testimony, through the prophets, prepared Paul for what was coming, that he might not be taken unawares. The verse displays the relation of inspiration and judgment: the prophets foretold truly, but the application — whether Paul should go — was his own Spirit-guided decision. The forewarning of bonds, far from deterring Paul, steeled him for the trial. The verse displays the providence that prepared the apostle: the Spirit witnessing of the afflictions ahead, that Paul might go forward with open eyes, surrendered to the will of God. The certainty of bonds, attested by the Spirit, sets the stage for Paul's noble resolve (v.24).
24:
"But none of these things move me, neither count I my life dear unto myself, so that I might finish my course with joy, and the ministry, which I have received of the Lord Jesus, to testify the gospel of the grace of God." The witnesses note Paul's noble resolve — his devotion above life itself. On "none of these things move me," the witnesses note his steadfastness. Ellicott compares Luther at Worms: "I will go thither, though there should be devils on every house-top." Paul was undeterred by the bonds foretold.

On "neither count I my life dear," the witnesses note the surrender of life to duty. Barnes: "Duty is of more importance than life... life is to be cheerfully surrendered." Paul valued not his life but the fulfillment of his ministry.

On "finish my course with joy," the witnesses note the textual question. Ellicott and Clarke note "with joy" is absent from many of the best MSS, perhaps a later insertion: Ellicott: "The passage is grander without them. What St. Paul desired was to finish his course." On "the gospel of the grace of God," the witnesses note the substance of his ministry (Robertson: "To Paul the gospel consisted in the grace of God").

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul declared "none of these things move me" — undeterred by the foretold bonds (like Luther at Worms, Ellicott), counting not his life dear, valuing duty above life (Barnes), desiring only to finish his course and ministry, testifying the gospel of the grace of God. The phrase "with joy" is textually doubtful, perhaps a later insertion (Ellicott, Clarke) — the passage grander without it. The verse displays Paul's noble resolve: his life surrendered to the completion of his ministry. The verse marks the height of the address: the apostle's devotion above life itself, his single aim to finish his course and testify the gospel of grace. The verse displays the model of the faithful servant: undeterred by danger, valuing duty above life, aiming only to complete the ministry received from Christ. Paul's resolve — to finish his course, whatever the cost — is the standard of Christian devotion. The verse displays the substance of his ministry: the gospel of the grace of God, the message to which his life was devoted. Paul's surrender of life to duty, his steadfastness in the face of bonds, and his single aim to testify the gospel of grace display the noblest pattern of ministry — a life held cheap that the course might be finished and the gospel proclaimed.
25:
"And now, behold, I know that ye all, among whom I have gone preaching the kingdom of God, shall see my face no more." The witnesses note Paul's solemn farewell — and debate whether he certainly foreknew it. The witnesses note the tender finality. Calvin: "what great force the words and speeches of men have which are uttered at their departure or death." Paul's words gained weight as a final farewell.

On whether Paul certainly foreknew, the witnesses divide but largely agree it was his expectation, not infallible prophecy. Clarke: "in his own mind, it was a matter of uncertainty." Ellicott: "The Apostle expressly disclaims foresight of his own future, and when he says, 'I know,' he speaks after the manner of men who take the fulfilment of their purpose for granted." Robertson notes the Pastoral Epistles show Paul did revisit Ephesus (1 Timothy 1:3), so "his fears turned out otherwise."

The witnesses note the purpose of the saying. Wesley: "He wisely inserts this, that what follows might make the deeper impression." The expectation of never meeting again deepened the weight of Paul's charge.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul declared the elders would see his face no more — a solemn farewell gaining weight as a parting word (Calvin). They largely agree this was his expectation, not infallible foreknowledge: he spoke "after the manner of men" taking the fulfillment of his purpose for granted (Ellicott), and the Pastoral Epistles show he did revisit Ephesus (Robertson, 1 Timothy 1:3), so his fears proved otherwise. The saying deepened the impression of his charge (Wesley). The verse displays the tender finality of the farewell: Paul, expecting never to see them again, charges them solemnly. The verse marks the poignant moment of parting: the apostle's belief that this was his last meeting with the Ephesian church. The verse displays the question of Paul's foreknowledge: he spoke as a man certain of his purpose, not as an infallible prophet — and events proved his expectation, though sincere, was not absolute. The verse displays the weight of a farewell: words spoken at parting carry deep force, and Paul used this to impress his charge. Whether or not he saw them again, the expectation that he would not deepened the solemnity of his words. The verse displays the human reality of the apostle: speaking from his honest expectation, drawing the elders to heed his final charge. The poignant farewell, "ye shall see my face no more," lends gravity to all that follows.
26:
"Wherefore I take you to record this day, that I am pure from the blood of all men." The witnesses note Paul's solemn disclaimer of responsibility. On "I take you to record," the grammatical voices note the appeal to witness. Robertson: "I call to witness." Paul calls the elders to testify that he has been faithful, so that no man's destruction can be charged to him.

On "pure from the blood of all men," the witnesses note the prophetic image. Ellicott and Calvin cite Ezekiel 33:18 (and 3:18): the watchman who warns is clean of the blood of those who perish. Barnes: "if they should die the second death... he would not be to blame. He had discharged his duty." Paul, having faithfully warned, is guiltless.

The Reformed Calvin draws the charge to pastors: "unless they will be guilty of blood before the Lord, they must do what in them lieth to bring those which go astray into the way." The minister who fails to warn is guilty of the blood of the perishing.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul calls the elders to witness that he is pure from the blood of all men — the watchman's disclaimer (Ezekiel 33, Ellicott, Calvin), Paul being guiltless of any man's destruction, having faithfully discharged his duty (Barnes). Calvin draws the charge: the pastor who fails to warn is guilty of the blood of the perishing. The verse displays Paul's solemn appeal: his conscience clear, having warned all. The verse marks the prophetic disclaimer: like Ezekiel's watchman, Paul has warned, and so is clean of the blood of any who perish. The verse displays the awful responsibility of the minister: faithfulness clears him, but negligence makes him guilty of the blood of souls. Paul, having preached the whole counsel of God (v.27), can claim a clear conscience. The verse displays the standard for the elders: they must warn faithfully, lest they be guilty of the blood of the lost. Paul's appeal — pure from the blood of all — is both his vindication and their charge. The verse displays the seriousness of the pastoral office: souls are at stake, and the minister's faithfulness or negligence has eternal consequences. Paul's clear conscience, grounded in his faithful warning, is the model the elders must follow, that they too may be pure from the blood of all men.
27:
"For I have not shunned to declare unto you all the counsel of God." The witnesses note Paul's full proclamation of God's counsel. On "not shunned," the witnesses note Paul's courage and faithfulness. He did not hold back from fear of persecution or desire to please, but declared the whole truth. Chrysostom: "he who does not speak, has blood to answer for."

On "all the counsel of God," the witnesses note the comprehensiveness. Gill: "his revealed will in the Gospel, concerning the salvation of men by Jesus Christ, even the whole of the Gospel, every truth and doctrine of it." Paul preached the complete revealed will of God, withholding nothing necessary to salvation.

The witnesses note this is the ground of his clear conscience (v.26). Because he declared all the counsel of God, Paul is pure from the blood of all. The verse displays the full, faithful ministry that clears the conscience.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul did not shun to declare all the counsel of God — not holding back from fear or favor (Chrysostom: silence incurs blood-guilt), but preaching the whole revealed will of God concerning salvation in Christ (Gill), withholding nothing necessary. This full proclamation is the ground of his clear conscience (v.26): because he declared all, he is pure from the blood of all. The verse displays the comprehensiveness of Paul's preaching: the whole counsel of God, not a partial or selective gospel. The verse marks the basis of the apostle's vindication: full faithfulness in declaring God's truth. The verse displays the standard for the elders: to preach the whole counsel of God, not shrinking from hard truths or unpopular doctrines. Paul's example — declaring all, shunning nothing — is the pattern of the faithful minister. The verse displays the link between full proclamation and a clear conscience: the minister who declares all the counsel of God is guiltless; the one who withholds is guilty of blood. The verse displays the wholeness of the gospel Paul preached: not a reduced or selective message, but the complete revealed will of God. The elders are charged to do likewise — to declare the whole counsel of God, that they too may be pure from the blood of all men. Paul's full, faithful ministry is the standard and the vindication.
28:
"Take heed therefore unto yourselves, and to all the flock, over the which the Holy Ghost hath made you overseers, to feed the church of God, which he hath purchased with his own blood." The witnesses note the great charge — and the famous textual and theological cruxes. On "take heed unto yourselves," the witnesses note the first duty. Calvin: "that man will never be careful for the salvation of other men who will neglect his own." The minister must first watch over himself.

On "overseers" (episkopous = bishops), the witnesses note the identity of bishop and elder. Calvin: "according to the use of the Scripture bishops differ nothing from elders." The elders (v.17) are here called bishops — one office, two names.

On "the church of God... his own blood," the witnesses note two cruxes. (1) The textual variant: "church of God" vs "church of the Lord." Barnes and Clarke favor "the Lord" (the older reading); Robertson, Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley favor "God." (2) "His own blood" — if "God," then "the blood of God." Calvin explains by the communicatio idiomatum: "Paul doth attribute blood to God; because the man Jesus Christ, who shed his blood for us, was also God" — the unity of the two natures in one person, against Nestorius and Eutyches. Clarke, holding "God," notes the alternative "by his own [Son]," but affirms the deity of Christ either way.

The witnesses agree: Paul charges the elders to take heed to themselves and the flock, over which the Holy Spirit made them overseers (bishops = elders, one office, Calvin), to feed the church of God, purchased with his own blood. The cruxes: the text reads "church of God" (Robertson, Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley) or "of the Lord" (Barnes, Clarke favor the older reading); and "his own blood," if "God," means the blood of God — explained by Calvin through the communicatio idiomatum (blood attributed to God because the man Christ who shed it was also God, the unity of person against Nestorius and Eutyches), Ellicott noting it sanctions calling Christ's blood "the blood of God." The verse displays the great charge: the elders to feed the church bought with Christ's blood. The verse marks the heart of the Miletus address: the pastoral charge grounded in the preciousness of the church (purchased with blood) and the dignity of the office (appointed by the Spirit). The verse displays both the textual question and the profound theology: the church of God, bought with the blood of him who is God — a witness to the deity of Christ and the value of his redemption. The charge — take heed to yourselves and the flock — rests on the costliness of the church: purchased with the blood of God himself. The verse displays the motive for faithful ministry: the church is precious beyond measure, bought with divine blood; to neglect it is sacrilege (Calvin). The elders, appointed by the Spirit, must feed the flock for which Christ shed his blood.
29:
"For I know this, that after my departing shall grievous wolves enter in among you, not sparing the flock." The witnesses note Paul's warning of wolves from without. On "grievous wolves," the grammatical voices note the false teachers and persecutors. Robertson: "Βαρεις is heavy, rapacious, harsh. Jesus had already so described false teachers who would raven the fold (John 10:12)." Whether Judaizers, Gnostics, or persecutors, dangerous enemies would assail the flock.

On "after my departing," the witnesses note the timing. Clarke refers it to Paul's death; others to his departure from them. Paul's presence had restrained the wolves; his absence would expose the flock. Calvin: "his presence was of great force to drive away wolves."

The Reformed Calvin notes the perennial danger: "This is a thing always incident to the Church to be hated of wolves. Wherefore, there is no time to sleep." The church is ever beset by enemies, requiring vigilant pastors.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul warns that after his departure grievous wolves — rapacious false teachers and persecutors (as Jesus foretold, John 10:12, Robertson) — will enter, not sparing the flock. His presence had restrained them (Calvin); his absence would expose the church. Calvin notes the perennial danger: the church is ever beset by wolves, requiring vigilance. The verse displays the warning of external enemies: dangerous teachers who would ravage the flock. The verse marks the prophetic warning of the address: the perils that would assail the Ephesian church after Paul's departure. The verse displays the reality of spiritual danger: the church is a flock among wolves, needing watchful shepherds. Paul's foresight — fulfilled in the heresies and persecutions that beset Ephesus (the Pastorals, Revelation 2) — displays his prophetic care. The verse displays the necessity of vigilance: the wolves will come, and the elders must guard the flock. The warning of grievous wolves, not sparing the flock, charges the elders to watchfulness. The verse displays the cost of the pastoral office: defending the flock against rapacious enemies, both false teachers and persecutors. Paul's warning prepares the elders for the dangers ahead, charging them to guard the church Christ purchased with his blood (v.28). The wolves from without (v.29) and the perverse men from within (v.30) together display the twofold peril that the vigilant shepherd must withstand.
30:
"Also of your own selves shall men arise, speaking perverse things, to draw away disciples after them." The witnesses note the warning of enemies from within. On "of your own selves," the witnesses note the inner danger. Barnes: "The most dangerous enemies which the church has had, have been nurtured in its own bosom." Ellicott names the later examples (Hymenaeus, Philetus, 1 Timothy 1:20; 2 Timothy 2:17). Heresy would arise from within the church.

On "speaking perverse things, to draw away disciples," the witnesses note the motive. Calvin: "ambition is the mother of all heresies... where men be desirous of mastership." Robertson: false teachers seek a following. The aim is to draw disciples after themselves, away from Christ.

The Reformed Calvin notes the schismatic root: "the sincerity of the word of God doth then flourish when the pastors join hand in hand to bring disciples unto Christ... where men be desirous of mastership" the doctrine is corrupted. Self-seeking ambition corrupts the gospel and divides the church.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul warns that even from among the elders themselves, men will arise speaking perverse things to draw away disciples — the most dangerous enemies nurtured within (Barnes), as later examples show (Hymenaeus, Philetus, Ellicott). Calvin notes the root: ambition, the desire for mastership, the mother of all heresies, drawing disciples after self rather than Christ. The verse displays the inner danger: heresy and schism arising from within the church. The verse marks the second peril: not only wolves from without (v.29) but perverse men from within, seeking a following. The verse displays the subtlety of the threat: enemies who were once disciples, now distorting the truth to gain a party. Paul's warning, fulfilled in the heresies of Ephesus, charges the elders to vigilance even among themselves. The verse displays the schismatic root of heresy: self-seeking ambition, the desire to draw disciples after oneself rather than to Christ. The pure ministry brings men to Christ alone; the corrupt ministry seeks its own following. The verse displays the standard for the elders: to bring disciples to Christ, not to themselves, guarding against the ambition that corrupts doctrine and divides the church. The twofold warning — wolves without (v.29), perverse men within (v.30) — charges the elders to watch (v.31) against every threat to the flock.
31:
"Therefore watch, and remember, that by the space of three years I ceased not to warn every one night and day with tears." The witnesses note the charge to watchfulness and Paul's example of tireless warning. On "watch," the grammatical voices note the echo of Christ's command. Ellicott: "an echo from our Lord's teaching (Matthew 24:42)... They who were the bishops, the overseers, the watchers of the flock, ought, above all others, to set an example of vigilance." The bishops must be wakeful.

On "three years," the witnesses note the round number for Paul's Ephesian ministry. Barnes and Ellicott note it covers the two years in Tyrannus's school (19:10), the three months in the synagogue (19:8), and the surrounding time — "three years, speaking roughly."

On "night and day with tears," the witnesses note Paul's tireless, affectionate warning. Barnes: "Expressive of his deep feeling." Robertson: "Paul kept it up with tears." The apostle warned every individual, continually, with tears — the model of pastoral diligence.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul charges the elders to watch (echoing Christ's command, Matthew 24:42, Ellicott — the overseers must be vigilant), remembering his own example: three years (his Ephesian ministry, roughly, Barnes) of ceaseless warning, night and day, with tears, to every individual. The verse displays the charge and the pattern: watchfulness, grounded in Paul's tireless, tearful example. The verse marks the climax of the warning: in view of the perils (vv.29-30), the elders must watch, as Paul had watched. The verse displays the model of pastoral diligence: warning every one, continually, with tears — not perfunctory but heartfelt and unceasing. Paul's three years of tearful warning set the standard for the elders' vigilance. The verse displays the affectionate intensity of true ministry: not cold duty but tears, not occasional warning but night and day, not the crowd in general but every individual. The charge to watch, grounded in Paul's example, calls the elders to the same tireless care. The verse displays the cost and the love of the pastoral office: ceaseless vigilance, heartfelt warning, tears for the flock. Paul's example — three years of night-and-day warning with tears — is the pattern the elders must follow to guard the church against the wolves and the perverse men. The verse charges watchfulness as the great duty of the shepherd.
32:
"And now, brethren, I commend you to God, and to the word of his grace, which is able to build you up, and to give you an inheritance among all them which are sanctified." The witnesses note Paul's commending of the elders to God. Chrysostom marks the comfort after the warning: "since he had much alarmed them by saying, 'Grievous wolves shall enter in among you,' therefore, not to overpower them... observe the consolation (he gives). 'I commend you to God, and to the word of His grace': that is, to His grace: it is grace that saveth."

On "the word of his grace," the witnesses note the means of building up. The gospel of grace is able to build them up and give the inheritance. Calvin: the word of grace is the foundation of hope, the fountain of joy, and the rule of walking (Henry, under v.28).

On "an inheritance among the sanctified," the witnesses note the hope of glory. Chrysostom: "he puts them in mind of the hope to come." The word of grace gives the inheritance among the saints — the final reward.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul commends the elders to God and to the word of his grace — a consolation after the alarming warning (Chrysostom), grace being what saves and builds up. The word of grace is able to build them up and give them an inheritance among the sanctified (the hope of glory, Chrysostom). The verse displays the apostle's confidence: committing the elders not to himself but to God and the gospel of grace. The verse marks the transition from warning to comfort: after the perils (vv.29-31), Paul points the elders to God and his word as their sufficiency. The verse displays the means of Christian growth and hope: the word of grace, able to build up and to give the inheritance. Paul, departing, leaves the elders in the hands of God and his gospel — a far surer keeping than his own presence. The verse displays the sufficiency of grace: the word of God's grace builds up the saints and secures their inheritance. The commending to God and the word displays Paul's trust: not in himself, nor in the elders' strength, but in God and his gospel. The verse displays the hope set before the church: an inheritance among all the sanctified, secured by the word of grace. Paul's farewell commendation — to God and the word of his grace — is the legacy he leaves the elders, the foundation of their hope and the means of their growth.
33:
"I have coveted no man's silver, or gold, or apparel." The witnesses note Paul's disclaimer of covetousness. Chrysostom marks the root removed: "He takes away that which is the root of evils, the love of money. 'Silver, or gold,' he says. He says not, I have not taken, but, not even 'coveted.'" Paul did not even desire others' money — a higher claim than mere honesty.

The grammatical voices note the example against the wolves. Paul's freedom from covetousness contrasts with the false teachers who would exploit the flock for gain. His disinterestedness vindicated his ministry.

The witnesses note the apostle's integrity. Having handled the collection (v.4) and labored among them, Paul could appeal to his complete freedom from greed — a powerful testimony and a charge to the elders against avarice.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul disclaimed all covetousness — not merely that he had not taken, but that he had not even coveted silver, gold, or apparel (Chrysostom removing the root of evils, the love of money). His disinterestedness contrasts with the false teachers who exploit the flock, and vindicates his ministry. The verse displays the apostle's integrity: free even from the desire for gain. The verse marks Paul's appeal to his freedom from greed: a higher claim than mere honesty — he had not even coveted. The verse displays the example against avarice: Paul, who had every right to support (1 Corinthians 9), took nothing and desired nothing, that the gospel might be free and his ministry above reproach. The disclaimer, far from boasting, is a charge to the elders: to be free from covetousness, the root of so much evil in ministry. The verse displays the contrast between the true and the false shepherd: the wolves devour the flock for gain; Paul coveted nothing. The verse displays the standard for the elders: not greed but disinterested service, not exploiting the flock but laboring freely. Paul's freedom from covetousness — not even coveting silver or gold — is the model of the unselfish minister, a rebuke to all who would make merchandise of the gospel. The verse charges the elders to the same purity: to serve the flock, not to fleece it.
34:
"Yea, ye yourselves know, that these hands have ministered unto my necessities, and to them that were with me." The witnesses note Paul's self-support by manual labor. Chrysostom marks the example: "Observe him employed in work and not simply that, but toiling. 'These hands have ministered unto my necessities, and to them that were with me': so as to put them to shame." Paul worked with his hands, supporting himself and his companions.

The grammatical voices note the tentmaking (18:3; 1 Corinthians 4:12). Paul labored at his trade, not burdening the church, supporting not only himself but his companions. This was a powerful testimony.
