The witnesses note the charge implied. By his own example of labor, Paul shames any greed and charges the elders to honest work and care for the weak (v.35). His toiling hands vindicate his disinterested ministry.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul appeals to his manual labor — "these hands" supporting himself and his companions (Chrysostom: toiling, to put them to shame), working at his tentmaking trade (18:3; 1 Corinthians 4:12) rather than burdening the church. The verse displays the apostle's self-support: laboring with his hands for his own needs and his companions'. The verse marks the proof of Paul's disinterestedness: not only did he covet nothing (v.33), but he worked to support himself. The verse displays the dignity of labor and the integrity of the apostle: his toiling hands vindicated his ministry and shamed any greed. Paul, who had the right to support, chose to work, that the gospel might be free and that he might set an example. The verse displays the model for the elders: honest labor, self-support where needed, and care for others. Paul's hands, ministering to his own necessities and his companions', display the unselfish, laborious ministry he commends. The verse displays the practical example behind the principle (v.35): Paul worked not only for himself but to support others, embodying the blessedness of giving. The apostle's manual labor — these very hands — is the visible proof of his freedom from greed and his care for the weak, a charge to the elders to labor and to give rather than to exploit the flock.
35:
"I have shewed you all things, how that so labouring ye ought to support the weak, and to remember the words of the Lord Jesus, how he said, It is more blessed to give than to receive." The witnesses note the charge to support the weak and the famous unrecorded saying of Jesus. On supporting the weak, Chrysostom notes the worthy aim: "Ye ought to show yourselves superior to money... 'to support the weak.'" Paul's labor was not only for himself but to help the needy.

On "the words of the Lord Jesus," the witnesses note this is an agraphon — a saying of Jesus not recorded in the Gospels. The puritan Henry: "he adds to it a saying of their Master's... 'It is more blessed to give than to receive': it seems they were words often used to his disciples." It is a precious saying preserved only here.

On the saying's meaning, the witnesses note its counter-worldly truth. Henry: "The opinion of the children of this world is contrary to this... but Christ tells us what is more blessed... It makes us more like to God, who gives to all, and receives from none." Giving, not getting, is the higher blessedness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul charges the elders, by his example of labor, to support the weak, and cites an unrecorded saying of Jesus (an agraphon, preserved only here): "It is more blessed to give than to receive" — words often used by Christ to his disciples (Henry). The saying expresses a counter-worldly truth: giving, not getting, is the higher blessedness, making us like God who gives to all (Henry). The verse displays the charge to generosity: laboring to support the weak, in imitation of Christ's own teaching. The verse marks the climax of Paul's example: not only self-support but giving to others, grounded in the Lord's own word. The verse displays the precious agraphon: a saying of Jesus not in the Gospels, treasured in the church's memory and recorded by Paul. The saying — more blessed to give than to receive — overturns the world's wisdom: the world counts getting blessed, but Christ counts giving the greater blessedness. The verse displays the standard for the elders: to labor and to give, supporting the weak, in the spirit of Christ. Paul's example and the Lord's saying together charge the church to generosity. The verse displays the heart of Christian liberality: giving is more blessed than receiving, for it makes us like God and like Christ, who gave himself. The agraphon, preserved by Paul, is one of the most cherished sayings of Jesus, the foundation of Christian generosity.
36:
"And when he had thus spoken, he kneeled down, and prayed with them all." The witnesses note Paul's prayer at the close of his address. The puritan Henry notes the fitness of parting in prayer: "It is good for friends, when they part, to part with prayer." Paul ends his charge by kneeling with the elders in prayer.

Chrysostom marks the feeling: "He prayed over them while exhorting them... he did not simply pray, but with much feeling." The prayer was fervent, sealing the exhortation.

On kneeling, the witnesses note the posture of humble, earnest prayer. Paul knelt with them all — a gesture of reverence and earnestness, commending them to God (v.32) in fervent supplication. The verse displays the prayerful close of the farewell.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, having finished his charge, knelt and prayed with the elders — the fitting close of a parting (good for friends to part with prayer, Henry), and a fervent prayer, with much feeling (Chrysostom). The kneeling marked humble, earnest supplication. The verse displays the prayerful end of the address: Paul commending the elders to God in fervent prayer. The verse marks the close of the Miletus discourse: the charge sealed with prayer. The verse displays the apostle's reverence and affection: kneeling with the elders, praying earnestly for them. The prayer fulfilled the commendation of v.32 — Paul committing the elders to God, now in actual supplication. The verse displays the model of Christian parting: not mere words but prayer, commending one another to God. Paul's kneeling prayer with the elders displays the depth of his love and the seriousness of the farewell. The verse, brief as it is, marks a tender moment: the apostle and the elders on their knees, praying together before the painful separation. The prayerful close displays the bond between Paul and the Ephesian leaders — a bond sealed in fervent supplication, commending them to the God and the word of grace able to keep them. Paul's prayer is the fitting end to his charge, committing the flock and its shepherds to the Lord.
37:
"And they all wept sore, and fell on Paul's neck, and kissed him," The witnesses note the weeping farewell of the elders. The grammatical voices note the depth of affection. Chrysostom: "so much did they love him, such was their affection towards him." The elders wept aloud, embraced Paul, and kissed him — a scene of intense love and grief.

On the embrace and kiss, the witnesses note the customary expression of affection (the holy kiss). The elders fell on Paul's neck, weeping, in the manner of deep love and sorrowful parting (as Joseph and his brethren, the patriarchs).

The witnesses note the cause of grief (v.38): chiefly that they should see his face no more. Their love for the apostle, who had served them three years with tears, broke out in weeping at the parting. The verse displays the affectionate bond between Paul and the elders.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the elders wept aloud, fell on Paul's neck, and kissed him — a scene of intense love and grief (such was their affection, Chrysostom), the embrace and kiss expressing deep affection in sorrowful parting. The cause was chiefly that they would see his face no more (v.38). The verse displays the depth of the bond between Paul and the Ephesian elders: tears, embraces, kisses at the parting. The verse marks the emotional climax of the farewell: the elders' overwhelming grief at losing their beloved apostle. The verse displays the fruit of Paul's loving ministry: the elders' intense affection, breaking out in weeping and embraces. The apostle who had served them with tears (v.31) is now wept over by them. The verse displays the tenderness of Christian fellowship: the painful parting of those bound in love, expressed in tears and embraces. The weeping farewell displays the reality of the bond — not formal but deeply affectionate, the elders loving Paul as a father. The verse displays the cost of love: the deeper the bond, the sharper the parting. The elders' tears, embraces, and kisses are the measure of their love for the apostle who had labored among them. The verse displays one of the most moving scenes in Acts: the weeping farewell at Miletus, the elders clinging to Paul before he sails to bonds and afflictions.
38:
"Sorrowing most of all for the words which he spake, that they should see his face no more. And they accompanied him unto the ship." The witnesses note the chief cause of grief and the escorting to the ship. On "most of all," the grammatical voices note what grieved them most. Chrysostom: "He had said, that 'grievous wolves should enter in'; had said, 'I am pure from the blood of all men': and yet the thing that grieved them most of all was this, 'that they should see him no more.'" The personal loss, not the warnings, grieved them most.

On accompanying him to the ship, the witnesses note the final act of love. The elders escorted Paul to the ship, unwilling to part, clinging to his presence to the last.

The witnesses note the poignancy of the scene. The deep love of the elders, their grief at the parting, and their escort to the ship display the bond between the apostle and the church. The verse closes the Miletus farewell with tender sorrow.

The thin comment matches the verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: the elders sorrowed most of all that they would see Paul's face no more (the personal loss grieving them more than the warnings of wolves, Chrysostom), and accompanied him to the ship — the final act of love, clinging to his presence to the last. The verse displays the chief cause of grief: not the perils foretold but the loss of the beloved apostle. The verse marks the poignant close of the Miletus farewell: the elders' deepest sorrow at the parting, and their escort to the ship. The verse displays the depth of love: what grieved them most was the personal separation, the never seeing his face again. The escorting to the ship displays their unwillingness to part, accompanying Paul to the very last. The verse closes the moving farewell with tender sorrow: the elders, having wept and embraced, walk with Paul to the ship, grieving above all at the final parting. The verse displays the human reality of Christian love and loss: the painful separation of those deeply bound, the grief sharpened by the expectation of never meeting again. The elders' sorrow and their escort display the fruit of Paul's ministry — a love so deep that the parting was the heaviest grief. The verse closes the great Miletus address and farewell, sending Paul on toward Jerusalem and the bonds awaiting him, accompanied to the ship by the weeping elders who loved him as a father.
